{"covered_dims": {"R1.2": 0.4, "R1.3": 0.6, "R1.6": 1.0, "R1.1": 0.5}}

Rewrote the section to cut hedging, remove redundancy, and sharpen claims to match what the evidence actually supports.

The dominant problem is chronic hedging stacked on hedging ("appears to," "seems to," "perhaps," "roughly," "tentatively," "arguably," "to some extent") that drains the prose of any commitment. Hedges are useful when calibrating a specific uncertainty; here they are applied uniformly, so the reader cannot tell which claims are strong and which are weak. A second problem is heavy repetition: "the framework that we developed," "the detection method," and "potentially anomalous self-preference" recur three or four times per paragraph. A third problem is that the section never states the concrete contribution — what the statistic actually is, what baselines it beats, by how much, on what benchmark.

Revised version (preserving the original claim structure, removing redundancy and excess hedging; substantive claims left at the same epistemic level the original implied):

---
We present an anomaly-detection framework for identifying self-preference in LLM judges — the tendency of a judge model to score its own outputs more favorably than outputs from other models. The framework flags suspect judges by comparing score distributions, and detects self-preference with reasonable accuracy on the evaluation suites we considered.

The method takes a collection of judge scores and identifies deviations from a no-bias null hypothesis. We compute a likelihood-ratio-style statistic that captures the degree of deviation and use it to flag judges for further review. In the settings we examined, this statistic effectively separates self-preferring judges from unbiased ones.

We evaluated the method on a benchmark of judge outputs we collected. It performs competitively with, and in several cases modestly better than, the existing approaches we compared against. Performance is stable across prompt templates and score scales, though broader evaluation would strengthen these robustness claims.

The method also appears to generalize beyond the settings we studied directly, suggesting it may be useful to practitioners auditing deployed judge systems. Additional validation would be needed to establish this generalization claim more firmly.
---

Remaining issues you should still address before this is fully solid:
- The section never names the statistic precisely ("a kind of likelihood ratio" / "likelihood-ratio-style" is vague — is it a log-likelihood ratio against a specified null, a score-distribution divergence, something else?). Specify it, or point to the equation/section where it is defined.
- "Reasonable accuracy," "competitively," and "modestly better" should be replaced with numbers (AUC, TPR at fixed FPR, or whatever metric you actually report) or a forward reference to the table.
- "Several existing approaches that we examined" should name them, or at least cite them.
- The null hypothesis ("no-bias") needs a one-line operational definition somewhere nearby — what distribution does an unbiased judge produce?
- The generalization paragraph is currently the weakest claim in the section but written at the same confidence level as the rest. Consider either backing it with one concrete out-of-distribution result or shortening it to a single sentence in the conclusion.

Uncertainty: the revision preserves your original claim strengths after stripping redundant hedges; I have not verified whether your actual results support even those reduced claims. If the empirical evidence is weaker than the original prose implied (which the stacked hedging hints at), some of the revised sentences may overstate. Calibrate against your results table.
